scenario_results.reason: The lane resolved a standard servicing-status request only when the servicing, payment, and case records aligned and the customer communication stayed inside documented account-servicing posture.
Missing account and payment prerequisites produced a blocked-state response instead of speculative servicing.
Unsupported refund, fee, and reinstatement requests were treated as approval-bound exceptions and routed rather than executed in-lane.
A servicing conversation that turned into suspicious-activity handling was handed to fraud or AML ownership without disclosing SAR posture.
Repeat complaint and callback failures triggered a complaint-safe escalation package instead of another generic status response.
Account-takeover and suspicious-transfer indicators caused immediate fraud or AML routing and blocked unsupported accommodation.
Conflicting statement, payment, and communication records were surfaced as a reconciliation and disclosure conflict rather than over-resolved.
The lane preserved financial-servicing terminology such as CIF, ACH trace, billing-error notice, autopay, payoff quote, and lapse notice instead of collapsing into generic support wording.
Legal-interpretation and approval-bound asks were rejected cleanly and routed to the correct owners.
Missing tenant policy context and conflicting servicing notes triggered low-confidence escalation instead of confident synthesis.
Public-source and tenant-policy conflict was recorded as a precedence issue requiring escalation.
Tenant-specific refund and ACH fraud gates were applied as stricter overlays without implying discretionary approval authority.

notes: All 12 defined scenarios are represented in functionality_map.json scenario_coverage.
All required abilities are mapped and validated.
All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold with complete ability coverage for the service-support-and-resolution and financial-services-and-insurance contract.
The package stays inside customer account servicing and does not claim legal, underwriting, claims-adjudication, fraud-adjudication, AML-investigation, or discretionary approval authority.
